paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 24: The Photograph on the MRT (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 4 girl who takes a photograph for an elderly Indian auntie on the East-West Line one Sunday afternoon, and only later understands what the auntie was pointing at through the window. Answer the questions that follow. Section B [19 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect). Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2] as in the real paper: 10 short-answer questions (Q1-Q10) — 2 literal, 3 vocabulary-in-context, 3 inference, 2 language-for-effect — plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q11). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
It was a Sunday afternoon in November and the East-West Line was almost empty. I had boarded at Tampines and was heading west, with a paperback book on my lap that I was not really reading. The carriage rocked gently. Through the window, HDB blocks slid past, each one looking very much like the next.

Paragraph 2
An elderly Indian auntie stepped in at Bedok and sat down beside me. She wore a cream-coloured sari with a thin green border, and she carried a small black handbag that she kept on her knees. She had with her one of those old flip phones with worn keys that I had not seen in years. She seemed to be looking for something on it.

Paragraph 3
'Excuse me, girl,' she said after a while, in lightly-accented English. 'Can you help me to take one photograph?' She held the phone out shyly, the way you might offer something you were not sure was worth troubling someone over. I closed my book and said yes, of course, auntie.

Paragraph 4
She stood up, steadied herself on the metal pole, and pressed her left hand flat against the window of the moving train. With her right hand she pointed past the glass, urgently, as though afraid I would not catch what she was pointing at in time. 'There,' she said. 'There. There.' I lifted the phone and took the photograph. I took two more, just in case.

Paragraph 5
She sat down and thanked me. She looked at the small screen for a long time, much longer than the photographs themselves deserved. Then she smiled at me and put the phone away into her handbag. Eunos slid past. Then Kembangan. We sat quietly. I picked up my book again and pretended to read.

Paragraph 6
After she alighted at Paya Lebar, I turned to the window myself. I tried to remember which direction she had been pointing. Past the carriage, beyond a row of low warehouses, was a wide, cleared rectangle of red earth fenced off with green hoarding. Three cranes stood still against the sky. There was nothing else there — just dust, and the sharp afternoon sun.

Paragraph 7
It was only later that evening, scrolling through the news at home, that I understood. The cleared land I had passed was the site of an old row of two-room HDB flats that had been demolished the previous month under the Selective En bloc Redevelopment Scheme. The article showed a photograph: a low block, paint flaking, with bicycles leaning along the corridor and laundry strung between the windows. It had a kampung-like feeling, the writer said. A community of mostly older residents had lived there for over forty years.

Paragraph 8
I thought about the auntie pressing her hand against the glass. I thought about the urgency of her 'there, there, there', and the long quiet moment she had spent looking at the screen afterwards. I had taken a photograph of an empty field. She had taken a photograph of her front door, of the corridor where her neighbours had hung their washing, of forty years of an address that no longer existed. I wondered, sitting at my desk, how many other windows I had passed without seeing.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. SERS / Selective En bloc Redevelopment Scheme: an HDB programme under which older blocks are demolished and residents resettled into newer flats; sari: a traditional Indian garment; alight: to get off a train or bus.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, on what day of the week and in which month did this journey take place? [1]
Q2. What does the word 'shyly' (Paragraph 3) suggest about how the auntie asked for help? [1]
Q3. Using your own words, explain how the writer shows that the auntie belongs to an older generation. (Paragraph 2) [2]
questions.answer: ① she wears a traditional sari; ② she uses an old, worn flip phone of a kind no longer seen and seems unsure how to work it

questions.stem: Q4. What is the effect of the writer describing the auntie pressing her 'left hand flat against the window' (Paragraph 4) rather than simply pointing? [2]
questions.answer: the physical, reaching gesture suggests she is trying to touch something now out of reach, making the moment far more tender and emotional than a plain point would be

questions.stem: Q5. What does the word 'urgently' (Paragraph 4) suggest about how important the photograph was to the auntie? [1]
questions.answer: it was pressing / deeply important to her

questions.stem: Q6. From Paragraph 6, how many cranes stood against the sky over the cleared land? [1]
Q7. Using your own words, explain why the auntie looked at the small screen 'much longer than the photographs themselves deserved' (Paragraph 5). [2]
questions.answer: ① the picture carried a deep personal meaning beyond what was actually visible; ② so she lingered over it, seeing in it something the narrator could not

questions.stem: Q8. What does the word 'urgency' (Paragraph 8) suggest the narrator now understands about the auntie's request? [1]
questions.answer: it was not casual but emotionally pressing

questions.stem: Q9. Using your own words, explain the contrast the narrator draws in Paragraph 8 between her own photograph and the auntie's photograph. [2]
questions.answer: ① the narrator had captured only an empty field; ② the auntie was photographing her former home and forty years of an address that had been demolished, so they saw the same place utterly differently

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of the final sentence, 'I wondered, sitting at my desk, how many other windows I had passed without seeing' (Paragraph 8)? [2]
questions.answer: the rhetorical reflection shows the narrator realising she has long moved past places that mattered to others without noticing, widening her sense of what is worth seeing

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts across the Sunday afternoon and that evening at her desk. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: helpful / curious / moved / reflective / amused / annoyed / proud / impatient. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Photograph on the MRT' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings move through four stages — from a willing but unthinking helpfulness as she takes the photograph for the auntie, to a quiet curiosity about what the auntie had actually been pointing at, to being deeply moved as the meaning of the photograph becomes clear that evening, ending in a reflective awareness of all that she has previously failed to see. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 3-4 as she closes her book and lifts the phone to take the photograph for the auntie? [1]
Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 as she turns to the window after the auntie has alighted and tries to remember which direction she had been pointing? [1]
Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7 as she reads the news article that evening and understands what the auntie had been photographing? [1]
Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as she sits at her desk wondering how many other windows she had passed without seeing? [1]
